summary: Fowler observes that UML 2's development was driven by MDA and programming language use cases, leaving sketchers—the majority of practitioners—dissatisfied. Surprisingly, even the MDA community itself shows disdain for UML, preferring simplified subsets. This creates an identity crisis for UML: sketchers find the standards increasingly irrelevant, while MDA tools fragment into proprietary dialects. Fowler questions whether UML can serve as a meaningful interchange mechanism when no community truly embraces the full standard.
key_points: UML 2 was designed for MDA and formal modeling, ignoring the needs of sketchers who use UML informally
Sketchers bear some responsibility for being ignored since they don't participate in UML committees
Even MDA proponents don't embrace full UML, preferring simplified or extended subsets
The fragmentation into tool-specific UML dialects threatens interoperability and portability
MOF may replace UML as the interchange mechanism, but practical portability remains uncertain
money_quotes: Most people I talk to are interested in UmlAsSketch and this group isn't very impressed with UML 2.
Sketchers were pretty much ignored. This was largely their own fault as sketchers aren't interested enough in the UML to take an active role in the UML committees.
Disdain for UML is pretty rampant amongst the UmlAsProgrammingLanguage community too.
The pro-MDA speakers based their assumptions on the fact that they would be using a simplified subset of UML, and emphasized that you should not judge MDA on the UML.
I hear more mutterings from sketchers about the growing irrelevance of UML standards.
Will users of MDA tools get portability in practice, or will each tool turn into its own proprietary language?
key_insight: When a standard tries to serve formal tooling needs while ignoring informal practitioners, it risks becoming a language that nobody actually wants to use as designed.
